claims.evidence_text: Fall counts of 80-100 tillers/sq ft indicate a strong stand and good potential for winter survival and yield.
claims.claim_summary: Fall tiller counts of 80-100 tillers per square foot indicate a strong wheat stand with good winter survival and yield potential.

claims.evidence_text: Fields with tillers between 80 and 50 may respond to nitrogen fall applications, but responses will be more variable and less predictable
claims.claim_summary: Wheat fields with 50-80 tillers per square foot in fall may respond variably to fall nitrogen applications.

claims.evidence_text: If less than 50 tillers per sq foot, a nitrogen application of 15-30 lbs/acre will encourage fall tillering.
claims.claim_summary: Below 50 tillers per square foot in fall, a nitrogen application is recommended to encourage tillering.

claims.evidence_text: If less than 50 tillers per sq foot, a nitrogen application of 15-30 lbs/acre will encourage fall tillering.
claims.claim_summary: A fall nitrogen application of 15-30 lb/acre is recommended for low-tiller wheat stands to encourage tillering.

claims.evidence_text: most wheat varieties benefit much more from spring nitrogen than fall nitrogen
claims.claim_summary: Most wheat varieties benefit more from spring nitrogen than fall nitrogen application.

claims.evidence_text: For fields with a yield potential of 75 to 90%, a first top-dressing application close to green-up, Feeks 3-4 to promote tillering... and a second top-dressing application around Feeks 6-8 to apply the remainder.
claims.claim_summary: For 75-90% yield potential wheat fields, split nitrogen: first top-dress at Feekes 3-4 (green-up), second at Feekes 6-8.

claims.evidence_text: For fields with a potential of 90% or greater, nitrogen should be applied between Feeks 3 and 6, with potential for a second application around Feeks 8
claims.claim_summary: For wheat fields with 90%+ yield potential, apply nitrogen between Feekes 3 and 6, with a possible second application near Feekes 8.

claims.evidence_text: the Pennsylvania Agronomy Guide recommends 1.0 lbs of nitrogen per bushel of expected wheat. A 100-bu/acre field needs 100 total nitrogen units.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat nitrogen requirement is recommended at 1.0 lb N per bushel of expected yield (total fertilizer plus soil N).

claims.evidence_text: If the field is averaging 19 plants per square foot, the yield potential is 90-95%. If the average is 10 plants per square foot, the yield potential will be closer to 55%.
claims.claim_summary: A wheat spring stand averaging 19 plants/sq ft gives 90-95% yield potential; 10 plants/sq ft gives ~55%.

claims.evidence_text: Table 2: ... Final Stand (%) 100 | Plants per sq. ft. 30-35 | Plants per sq. yd. 270-315 | Potential Yield 100
claims.claim_summary: A full (100%) wheat stand corresponds to 30-35 plants per square foot for 100% yield potential.

claims.evidence_text: Fields with stand counts below 15 plants per square foot have yield potential below 75 percent (Table 2) and should not be kept; instead, they should be rotated into corn or soybeans.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat fields below 15 plants/sq ft have under 75% yield potential and should be rotated out rather than kept.

claims.evidence_text: Fields with stand counts below 15 plants per square foot ... should not be kept; instead, they should be rotated into corn or soybeans.
claims.claim_summary: Poor wheat stands (<15 plants/sq ft) should be rotated into corn or soybeans.

claims.evidence_text: Consider drowned out in low-lying spots, heaving or the pushing of the plant out of the soil, and exposed roots. These problems are more prevalent in low areas and areas of high clay content.
claims.claim_summary: Winter frost heaving pushes wheat plants out of the soil exposing roots, more prevalent in low-lying, high-clay areas.

claims.evidence_text: Consider drowned out in low-lying spots... These problems are more prevalent in low areas and areas of high clay content.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat is subject to drowning out in low-lying waterlogged spots, especially in high-clay soils.

claims.evidence_text: if you count 80 plants in 2 feet, on 7.5-inch spacing, that equals 64 plants/sq foot.
claims.claim_summary: Example wheat row spacing of 7.5 inches used for converting row counts to plants per square foot.
